# Brooks-Lint — Full Sweep Report
**Mode:** Full Sweep
**Scope:** inline snippet — 2 lines (1 production class `Billing`, 1 test)
**Health Score:** 35/100

A 1500-line god class with five unrelated responsibilities is paired with a test that asserts nothing meaningful — both structure and safety net are compromised.

---

## Dimension Summary

| Dimension | Scanned | Safe Applied | Extended Applied | Reverted | Residual |
|-----------|---------|--------------|------------------|----------|----------|
| Review (R1–R6) | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 3 |
| Test (T1–T6)   | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| Debt           | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| Audit          | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

No project test/lint command was found for the inline snippet — verification step skipped; all structural fixes deferred to Residual per the Iron Law (no auto-refactor of module boundaries or public class shape).

---

## Iteration History

Round 1: mixed — 5 distinct findings, 0 auto-fixable (all require human judgment on module boundaries / public class shape).
Stopped at: no auto-fixable findings — nothing to re-scan.

---

## Findings

### 🔴 Critical

**Cognitive Overload (R1) — 1500-line god class mixes five responsibilities**
Symptom: `class Billing` packs `charge()`, `refund()`, `invoice()`, `dunning()`, and `report()` into a single 1500-line unit — well past the >50-line critical threshold, with five distinct concerns in one type.
Source: A Philosophy of Software Design — Shallow/God Module (Ch. 4); Code Complete — Ch. 7 High-Quality Routines.
Consequence: No one can hold the class in working memory; every reader pays the full 1500-line tax to touch any one method, and changes to billing silently risk breaking reporting.
Remedy: Extract each responsibility into its own deep module — `Charger`, `Refunder`, `Invoicer`, `DunningPolicy`, `Reporter` — coordinated by a thin `Billing` facade. Move method by method behind characterization tests.

**Change Propagation (R2) — single class changes for five unrelated business reasons**
Symptom: `Billing` changes when charging rules, refund rules, invoicing, dunning, or reporting change — five independent axes of change collapsed into one class (Divergent Change).
Source: Refactoring — Divergent Change; Clean Architecture — Single Responsibility Principle.
Consequence: Every billing-related change forces re-reading and re-testing all five concerns; merge conflicts and regression blast radius scale with team size (Brooks's communication overhead).
Remedy: Split along the five reasons-to-change so each concern lives in a class with one reason to change; the report logic in particular should not share a unit with money-movement code.

### 🟡 Warning

**Coverage Illusion (T5) — single test exercises one method and asserts nothing**
Symptom: `test("billing", …)` calls `b.charge()` then asserts only `expect(b).toBeTruthy()` — an object that always exists. `refund`, `invoice`, `dunning`, and `report` have zero coverage; no return value, side effect, or error path is verified.
Source: How Google Tests Software — change coverage vs line coverage; Osherove — test completeness principle.
Consequence: The suite reports "billing is tested" while protecting against nothing — the entire 1500-line class can be broken and this test still passes, the exact failure mode that makes coverage numbers lie.
Remedy: Replace `toBeTruthy()` with assertions on `charge()`'s actual outcome (charged amount, state transition, or emitted event), and add at least one behavioral test per public method before any refactor.

**Architecture Mismatch / Missing Seams (T6) — god class has no seams for safe change**
Symptom: A 1500-line class under active concern offers no interfaces, dependency injection, or seams, and has no characterization tests capturing current behavior before the extraction it badly needs.
Source: Working Effectively with Legacy Code — Seam Model (Ch. 4); Characterization Tests (Ch. 13).
Consequence: The class cannot be tested in isolation or refactored safely — the one test that exists does not pin behavior, so any split risks silent regressions with no detector.
Remedy: Write characterization tests around each method's observable behavior first, introduce seams (extract interfaces / inject collaborators), then decompose under that safety net.

### 🟢 Suggestion

**Test Obscurity (T1) — test name does not describe a scenario or outcome**
Symptom: The test is named `"billing"` — it expresses neither the scenario exercised nor the expected outcome.
Source: xUnit Test Patterns — Assertion Roulette-adjacent obscurity; The Art of Unit Testing — method_scenario_expected naming.
Consequence: A failure tells a maintainer nothing about what broke; the name invites duplicate "billing" tests rather than discoverable, behavior-named cases.
Remedy: Rename to a `method_scenario_expected` form, e.g. `charge_validCard_recordsPayment`, once a real assertion exists.

---

## Fix Log

(No fixes applied — all findings are Residual: each requires splitting a public class or authoring tests around unpinned legacy behavior, which falls outside Safe / Extended-Safe auto-fix criteria.)

---

## Health Score Delta
Before: 35/100  →  After: 35/100
(No in-place fixes were safe to apply; re-run /brooks-health for an exact recalculation.)

## Residual Items  (5 not applied)

🔴 **R1** — god class mixes five responsibilities → extract five deep modules behind a `Billing` facade. Not applied because: cross-module structural change + no test coverage to fall back on.

🔴 **R2** — one class, five reasons to change → split along reasons-to-change. Not applied because: public API restructuring + no test coverage.

🟡 **T5** — test asserts only `toBeTruthy()`, four methods uncovered → add behavioral assertions per public method. Not applied because: requires new test authoring (Residual, not a Safe scaffold of a pure function).

🟡 **T6** — no seams, no characterization tests before refactor → add characterization tests + extract seams. Not applied because: requires new test infrastructure.

🟢 **T1** — test named `"billing"` → rename to `method_scenario_expected`. Not applied because: renaming is meaningful only once a real assertion exists (coupled to the T5 fix).

## Summary
- Total findings detected: 5
- Fixed this sweep: 0
- Residual (needs human review): 5
- Unresolvable (3-retry exhausted): 0

The single highest-leverage move is to write characterization tests around `Billing`'s five methods (T5/T6) — without that safety net the structural split (R1/R2) cannot be done safely. Both the production structure and its test are decayed in a mutually reinforcing way: the god class is hard to test, and the empty test gives false confidence that it is fine, so neither pressure surfaces on its own.

## Residual Items  (3 not applied)

### 🟡 Warning

**Cognitive Overload (R1) — 10-parameter list with all params but one unused**
Symptom: `function fmt(d, opts, locale, tz, cal, num, sep, pad, zero, ampm)` declares 10 parameters; only `d` is used in the body.
Source: Refactoring — Long Parameter List.
Consequence: Callers must supply (or pass `undefined` for) 9 positional arguments with no compiler help on ordering; every call site is a chance to transpose `sep`/`pad`/`zero` silently, and the signature lies about what the function actually consumes.
Remedy: Reduce the signature to what is used — `function fmt(d)` — or, if the other options are genuinely coming, collapse them into a single `opts` object (`fmt(d, { locale, tz, cal, ... })`) so arguments are named and order-independent.
Not applied because: public API break — `fmt` is an exported-style top-level function and changing its arity alters every caller's contract; no test coverage to fall back on.

### 🟢 Suggestion

**Cognitive Overload (R1) — Magic number `86400000`**
Symptom: `Math.floor(d / 86400000)` divides by an unexplained literal; the comment flags it but does not name it.
Source: Code Complete — Ch. 12: Fundamental Data Types (magic numbers).
Consequence: A reader must recognize `86400000` as milliseconds-per-day to understand that `fmt` converts an epoch-ms value to a day index; the intent is invisible and the constant cannot be reused or unit-checked.
Remedy: Extract a named constant — `const MS_PER_DAY = 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;` — and return `Math.floor(d / MS_PER_DAY)`. (Single-file, leaf, non-exported — would normally be Safe, but see "Not applied" below.)
Not applied because: no test coverage — there is no characterization test pinning the current return value, so even this low-risk extraction cannot be verification-gated per Step 2e; recorded for human review.

**Cognitive Overload (R1) — Non-descriptive parameter name `d`**
Symptom: The single used parameter is named `d`; its meaning (an epoch-millisecond timestamp) is only inferable from the `86400000` division.
Source: Code Complete — Ch. 11: The Power of Variable Names.
Consequence: Maintainers must read the implementation to learn that `d` is milliseconds, not a `Date` object or a seconds value — a likely source of unit-mismatch bugs at call sites.
Remedy: Rename to convey type and unit, e.g. `epochMs`, and have the body operate on it (`Math.floor(epochMs / MS_PER_DAY)`).
Not applied because: no test coverage — rename is local but unverifiable without a pinning test; carried to residual per Step 2e.
